disease.name: Gallstone Pancreatitis
disease.note: Acute pancreatitis triggered by passage of a gallstone through the ampulla of Vater. Most common cause of acute pancreatitis worldwide. Ranges from mild self-limiting disease to severe necrotizing pancreatitis with multi-organ failure. Glasgow score (Imrie) ≥3 indicates severe disease. In Kenya, gallstone pancreatitis is rising with increased cholelithiasis.
presentingComplaints: Severe upper abdominal pain going through to the back
Pain that is better when sitting up or leaning forward
pathophysiologyDetail: Gallstone migration: a gallstone (usually 3-8 mm) passes from the gallbladder through the cystic duct into the common bile duct; as it traverses the ampulla of Vater, it causes transient obstruction of the pancreatic duct → ductal hypertension → triggers pancreatitis; the stone may pass into the duodenum or become impacted (causing persistent obstruction)

Premature trypsinogen activation: the key initiating event in acute pancreatitis — trypsinogen is activated to trypsin within pancreatic acinar cells (normally occurs only in the duodenum); trypsin then activates other pancreatic zymogens (chymotrypsinogen, proelastase, procarboxypeptidase, phospholipase A2) → autodigestion of pancreatic parenchyma

Intracellular trypsin activation mechanisms: (1) impaired trypsin inhibition (SPINK1 mutations decrease pancreatic secretory trypsin inhibitor), (2) impaired trypsin degradation (CFTR mutations affecting ductal bicarbonate secretion), (3) defective autophagy (mTOR pathway dysregulation in alcohol-induced pancreatitis), (4) calcium overload (CCK hyperstimulation causes sustained [Ca2+]i elevation → mitochondrial dysfunction → ATP depletion → necrosis)

Acinar cell injury pathways: sustained intracellular calcium elevation → mitochondrial permeability transition pore opening → ATP depletion → necrosis (not apoptosis) of acinar cells; necrosis releases damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs: HMGB1, ATP, DNA, histones) → triggers inflammatory cascade

Inflammatory cascade: DAMPs activate macrophages and neutrophils via TLRs and NLRP3 inflammasome → release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, IL-8) → neutrophil recruitment to pancreas → neutrophil elastase and reactive oxygen species → further tissue damage → propagation of inflammation

Local complications: (1) acute peripancreatic fluid collection (APFC) — early, <4 weeks, no defined wall; (2) pancreatic pseudocyst — >4 weeks, well-defined wall, amylase-rich fluid; (3) acute necrotic collection (ANC) — <4 weeks, containing necrotic tissue; (4) walled-off necrosis (WON) — >4 weeks, encapsulated necrotic tissue. Infected necrosis (30-50% of necrotizing pancreatitis) carries high mortality

Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS): pancreatic inflammation spills into systemic circulation → widespread endothelial activation → capillary leak → third-spacing of fluids → hypovolemia; compensatory anti-inflammatory response syndrome (CARS) may follow → immunosuppression → increased risk of secondary infection

Organ failure: (1) respiratory — most common; increased pulmonary capillary permeability → ARDS → hypoxemia; pleural effusions (left > right) from diaphragmatic inflammation; (2) renal — from hypovolemia, endotoxemia, abdominal compartment syndrome; (3) cardiovascular — vasoplegia, myocardial depression from cytokines; persistent organ failure >48h defines severe pancreatitis

Glasgow (Imrie) score components (within 48h): age >55, WBC >15 ×10⁹/L, glucose >10 mmol/L, urea >16 mmol/L, PaO2 <60 mmHg, calcium <2.0 mmol/L, albumin <32 g/L, LDH >600 IU/L. Score ≥3 predicts severe pancreatitis. Modified Glasgow: same 8 criteria, assessed at 48h. More commonly used in UK/Europe

Revised Atlanta Classification (2012) defines severity: Mild — no organ failure, no local complications; Moderately Severe — transient organ failure (<48h) and/or local complications; Severe — persistent organ failure (>48h) ± local complications. 80% are mild, 15% moderate, 5% severe

Ranson's criteria (historically important, now less used): assessed at admission (age, WBC, glucose, LDH, AST) and at 48h (Hct drop, BUN rise, calcium, PaO2, base deficit, fluid sequestration). Score 0-2: 1% mortality; 3-4: 15%; 5-6: 40%; ≥7: >90%. SIRS criteria at 24h has replaced Ranson for early prediction

Gallstone pancreatitis vs alcoholic pancreatitis: gallstone etiology more common in women, older patients, and those with known gallstones; ALT >150 U/L within 48h of onset is highly specific for gallstone etiology (specificity 95%, PPV 90%); alcoholic pancreatitis associated with chronic calcific pancreatitis, younger males, heavy alcohol use, lower ALT, more often recurrent

East African context: gallstone pancreatitis is increasing with rising cholelithiasis (dietary transition, obesity); alcoholic pancreatitis from busaa/chang'aa consumption remains common in men; hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis may be under-recognized; late presentation (>48h from pain onset) is frequent, making organ failure more common; access to ICU and CT is limited; mortality from severe pancreatitis is higher (15-25% vs 5-10% in high-income settings)

Recurrent acute pancreatitis: 20-30% of patients with acute pancreatitis have recurrences; investigations after second episode include MRCP/EUS for microlithiasis/sludge, IgG4 for autoimmune pancreatitis, genetic testing (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR) in young patients with family history; biliary sludge/microlithiasis is the most common cause of idiopathic recurrent acute pancreatitis

Hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis: triglycerides >1000 mg/dL (11.3 mmol/L) → chylomicrons cause microcirculatory sludging in pancreatic capillaries → ischemia; management: insulin infusion + IV fluids + heparin (controversial) + plasmapheresis if severe; higher risk of ICU admission; recurrent unless triglycerides controlled; fibrates (fenofibrate) for long-term prevention

landmarkTrials.trial: Aggressive vs Moderate Fluid Resuscitation in Acute Pancreatitis (2022, NEJM — WATERFALL)
landmarkTrials.population: 249 patients with acute pancreatitis randomized to aggressive vs moderate IV fluid resuscitation
landmarkTrials.intervention: Aggressive: 20 mL/kg bolus + 3 mL/kg/h Ringer's lactate vs Moderate: 10 mL/kg bolus + 1.5 mL/kg/h Ringer's lactate
landmarkTrials.outcome: Aggressive group: higher fluid overload (20.5% vs 6.3%, p<0.001), no difference in mortality or organ failure; moderate resuscitation is safer and non-inferior. Aggressive fluid resuscitation in acute pancreatitis is harmful, not beneficial.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Moderate fluid resuscitation is safer and more practical in resource-limited Kenyan ICUs

landmarkTrials.trial: Early vs Delayed Feeding in Acute Pancreatitis (2020, Clinical Gastroenterology & Hepatology — PYTHON Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: Patients with predicted severe acute pancreatitis randomized to early (within 24h) vs delayed (72h) oral feeding
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early oral feeding vs delayed oral feeding after resolution of symptoms
landmarkTrials.outcome: Delayed feeding: lower rate of intolerance (30% vs 45%), no difference in infection, organ failure, or mortality. Feeding should be initiated only when pain has resolved and bowel function returned. Routine early feeding does not benefit.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Feeding when pain resolved (often 3-5 days) is the standard in Kenyan practice

landmarkTrials.trial: Prophylactic Antibiotics in Necrotizing Pancreatitis (2004, Gastroenterology — Meta-analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of RCTs on prophylactic antibiotics in severe/necrotizing pancreatitis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics (imipenem, meropenem) vs no prophylaxis in necrotizing pancreatitis
landmarkTrials.outcome: No reduction in mortality, infected necrosis, or need for surgery with prophylactic antibiotics. Antibiotics increased risk of fungal superinfection. Current recommendation: antibiotics only when infection is proven or strongly suspected (not prophylactic).
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Antibiotics only for proven/suspected infection — important in Kenya to avoid unnecessary use and resistance

landmarkTrials.trial: Early ERCP in Gallstone Pancreatitis (2002, JAMA — Systematic Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of RCTs comparing early ERCP (<24-72h) vs conservative management in gallstone pancreatitis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early ERCP with sphincterotomy vs conservative management
landmarkTrials.outcome: Early ERCP reduced complications in patients with predicted severe pancreatitis (OR 0.44) and cholangitis (OR 0.27). No benefit in predicted mild pancreatitis without cholangitis. Routine ERCP in all gallstone pancreatitis is not indicated.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: ERCP indicated for cholangitis or predicted severe with biliary obstruction; limited ERCP access in Kenya

landmarkTrials.trial: Step-Up Approach vs Open Necrosectomy for Necrotizing Pancreatitis (2010, NEJM — PANTER)
landmarkTrials.population: 88 patients with necrotizing pancreatitis and suspected/confirmed infected necrosis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Step-up approach (percutaneous drainage → endoscopic necrosectomy → video-assisted retroperitoneal debridement) vs open necrosectomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Step-up approach: lower major complications (40% vs 70%), lower incisional hernias (7% vs 24%), lower new onset diabetes (16% vs 38%), and lower costs. Open necrosectomy should be reserved for failures of step-up. Major paradigm change in management.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Step-up approach requires interventional radiology expertise limited in Kenya; open necrosectomy still commonly performed

landmarkTrials.trial: Cholecystectomy Timing After Gallstone Pancreatitis (2018, BMJ — PONCHO Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 266 patients with mild gallstone pancreatitis randomized to same-admission vs interval cholecystectomy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy during same admission (within 72h of resolution) vs interval cholecystectomy after 4-6 weeks
landmarkTrials.outcome: Same-admission: fewer recurrent biliary events (5% vs 17%, p=0.002), no increase in operative difficulty or complications. Same-admission cholecystectomy is recommended for mild gallstone pancreatitis to prevent recurrence.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Same-admission cholecystectomy recommended; feasible in Kenyan practice when resources available

landmarkTrials.trial: Enteral vs Parenteral Nutrition in Severe Pancreatitis (2008, JAMA — Meta-analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: RCTs comparing enteral vs parenteral nutrition in severe acute pancreatitis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Enteral nutrition (nasogastric/nasojejunal) vs total parenteral nutrition (TPN)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Enteral nutrition: reduced mortality (RR 0.50), reduced multi-organ failure (RR 0.55), reduced infections (RR 0.47), lower cost. Nasogastric is as effective as nasojejunal (easier placement). TPN should be avoided unless enteral route is impossible.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Enteral nutrition is cheaper and more practical in Kenya; NG tube placement widely available vs NJ tube requiring radiology

landmarkTrials.trial: BISAP Score for Mortality Prediction (2009, Clinical Gastroenterology & Hepatology — Validation)
landmarkTrials.population: 185,256 patients with acute pancreatitis in a US database
landmarkTrials.intervention: BISAP score (BUN >25, Impaired mental status, SIRS, Age >60, Pleural effusion) — 5-point score at admission
landmarkTrials.outcome: Mortality: 0 points: 0.1%; 1: 0.5%; 2: 1.9%; 3: 5.8%; 4: 12.7%; 5: 22.5%. BISAP has similar accuracy to Ranson and APACHE II but is simpler and available at admission. Widely used for early risk stratification.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: BISAP is simple and applicable in Kenyan practice with limited lab resources

evidenceTable.therapy: Aggressive vs moderate IV fluids in acute pancreatitis (RCT)
Early vs delayed feeding in predicted severe (RCT)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Start oral feeding after pain resolution
ERCP only for cholangitis or predicted severe with obstruction

evidenceTable.therapy: Step-up vs open necrosectomy in infected necrosis (RCT)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Step-up approach preferred; open necrosectomy as rescue

evidenceTable.therapy: Enteral vs parenteral nutrition in severe pancreatitis (Meta-analysis)
evidenceTable.effect: RR 0.50 for mortality; RR 0.55 for organ failure; RR 0.47 for infections
evidenceTable.recommendation: Enteral nutrition preferred; TPN only if enteral impossible

evidenceTable.therapy: BISAP score for mortality prediction (Validation cohort)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Use BISAP score at admission for early risk stratification

evidenceTable.population: Severe acute pancreatitis needing enteral nutrition
evidenceTable.effect: No difference in tolerance or outcomes; NG easier and cheaper
evidenceTable.recommendation: Nasogastric feeding if tolerated; NJ if NG not tolerated

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical diagnosis — acute onset of severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, relieved by leaning forward, accompanied by nausea/vomiting; history of gallstones or alcohol consumption; pain typically builds over 1-2 hours and peaks rapidly

Step 2: Serum lipase — test of choice (more sensitive and specific than amylase); lipase >3× upper limit of normal confirms acute pancreatitis; lipase remains elevated longer than amylase (7-14 days vs 3-5 days); amylase may be normal in late presentation or alcoholic pancreatitis

Step 3: ALT level — ALT >150 U/L within 48h of onset is highly specific for gallstone etiology (PPV 90%); ALT <50 makes gallstone cause less likely. Combine with bilirubin and ALP to assess for persistent biliary obstruction

Step 4: Severity assessment at admission — BISAP score: BUN >25 mg/dL, Impaired mental status, SIRS criteria (≥2 of: HR >90, RR >20 or PaCO2 <32, Temp >38°C or <36°C, WBC >12 or <4), Age >60, Pleural effusion. Score ≥3 = predicted severe disease

Step 5: SIRS assessment — presence of SIRS at 24h is the strongest predictor of severe pancreatitis. Persistent SIRS (>48h) associated with organ failure in 50%. Resolution of SIRS within 48h predicts mild course. Check SIRS criteria at admission, 24h, and 48h

Step 6: Abdominal ultrasound — within 24-48h to confirm gallstones, CBD dilation, assess gallbladder wall; may be limited by ileus (bowel gas obscuring pancreas); if negative for gallstones and alcohol history absent, consider MRCP/EUS for microlithiasis, sludge, or pancreas divisum

Step 7: CT abdomen with IV contrast — DO NOT perform in first 48h unless diagnosis is uncertain or complication suspected (contrast may worsen early pancreatic necrosis, per some evidence, but more practically, it rarely changes early management). Optimal timing: 72-96h after admission if predicted severe or not improving. CT severity index (CTSI) predicts outcome

Step 8: CT severity index (Balthazar score): Grade A: normal (0); B: focal/diffuse enlargement (1); C: intrinsic pancreatic abnormalities with peripancreatic inflammation (2); D: single acute fluid collection (3); E: ≥2 fluid collections or gas in/adjacent to pancreas (4). Plus necrosis: none (0), <30% (2), 30-50% (4), >50% (6). Total 0-10. Score ≥7: mortality 17%, morbidity 92%

Step 9: MRCP if gallstone etiology suspected but US negative, or if biliary obstruction persists after pancreatitis resolves; MRCP can detect microlithiasis, sludge, pancreas divisum, and small CBD stones missed by US

Step 10: EUS for suspected microlithiasis — more sensitive than MRCP for sludge and microlithiasis (<3 mm stones); EUS-guided sampling of pancreatic cystic lesions if needed; indicates need for cholecystectomy even if transabdominal US is negative for gallstones

Kenya adaptation: lipase and amylase available at most labs; US widely available; CT at major centers (MRI limited); MRCP available at select tertiary centers; ERCP at 5-6 centers; BISAP score feasible with basic labs; SIRS assessment requires only vital signs and WBC — universally applicable
managementPearls: Fluid resuscitation: use Ringer's lactate (preferred over Normal Saline — lower risk of hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis and SIRS exacerbation). Initiate at 1.5 mL/kg/h (moderate rate). No bolus unless hypotensive. Reassess every 6h using clinical targets: HR <120, MAP >65, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h, Hct <44%. STOP fluids if signs of volume overload develop

Pain management: IV paracetamol 1g 6-hourly is first-line. Add IV opioids (morphine or pethidine) if inadequate. PCA opioid analgesia for severe pain. Avoid NSAIDs (risk of AKI in volume-depleted patients). There is no evidence that morphine worsens pancreatitis (old myth — morphine does NOT cause Sphincter of Oddi spasm sufficient to exacerbate pancreatitis)

Nutrition: early enteral nutrition (within 24-48h) in predicted severe pancreatitis reduces mortality and infections. Nasogastric tube is as effective as nasojejunal (easier to place). Start with a standard polymeric feed at 20 mL/h, increase by 20 mL/h every 4-6h as tolerated. TPN should be avoided unless enteral route is absolutely contraindicated (e.g., bowel perforation, bowel obstruction)

Indications for ERCP in gallstone pancreatitis: (1) concurrent cholangitis — URGENT ERCP within 24h; (2) persistent biliary obstruction (bilirubin rising or not falling after 48-72h); (3) predicted severe pancreatitis with evidence of biliary obstruction. Do NOT do ERCP routinely for all gallstone pancreatitis — it increases complications without benefit in mild disease

Cholecystectomy timing: (1) Mild pancreatitis — same admission (within 72h of resolution of pain/tolerance of oral diet) to prevent recurrent biliary events (NNT 9 for PONCHO trial); (2) Severe/necrotizing pancreatitis — delay at least 6-8 weeks until the acute necrotic collection has resolved or stabilized; earlier surgery in necrotizing disease increases mortality

Antibiotics: do NOT use prophylactic antibiotics in severe acute pancreatitis — they do not prevent infected necrosis and promote fungal superinfection. Indications for antibiotics: (1) proven infected necrosis (CT-guided FNA + Gram stain/culture), (2) gas in necrotic collection on CT (pathognomonic for infection), (3) persistent signs of sepsis beyond 7-10 days with negative cultures from other sources

When to suspect infected necrosis: clinical deterioration after initial improvement, persistent fever beyond 7 days, new organ failure after the first week, increasing inflammatory markers, gas on CT within necrotic collection. Treatment: CT-guided FNA for culture → targeted antibiotics → step-up drainage (percutaneous drain → endoscopic transmural drainage → VARD/necrosectomy) if not improving

Abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS): consider in severe pancreatitis with increasing abdominal distension, oliguria, and elevated bladder pressure >20 mmHg in the setting of new organ failure. Management: stop NG feeds, NGT suction, neuromuscular blockade, percutaneous drainage of ascites if present; if no improvement, consider decompressive laparotomy (high mortality but life-saving)

Management of hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis: triglycerides >11.3 mmol/L (1000 mg/dL). Start insulin infusion (0.1-0.3 U/kg/h as insulin drip) with 5% dextrose to maintain euglycemia. Heparin (unfractionated 5000 U bolus then 1000 U/h) — controversial but may help. Plasmapheresis if severe with persistent hypertriglyceridemia. Long-term: fibrate therapy (fenofibrate) + dietary fat restriction

Post-pancreatitis follow-up: check for exocrine insufficiency (fecal elastase-1, clinical steatorrhea) and endocrine insufficiency (fasting glucose, HbA1c) at 4-6 weeks after discharge; up to 30% develop new diabetes after acute necrotizing pancreatitis; pancreatic enzymes if steatorrhea develops; alcohol counseling and smoking cessation; cholecystectomy if gallstone etiology

In Kenyan practice, where ICU beds and CT are limited, the management priorities are: (1) early risk stratification using BISAP/SIRS, (2) moderate IV fluid resuscitation with Ringer's lactate, (3) early enteral nutrition by NG tube, (4) no prophylactic antibiotics, (5) transfer patients with predicted severe pancreatitis to a facility with ICU and CT capacity, (6) ERCP only for cholangitis or persistent obstruction
operativeNuances: Cholecystectomy timing for mild pancreatitis: perform during the same admission once: (1) abdominal pain has completely resolved, (2) oral diet is tolerated without recurrence of pain, (3) inflammatory markers (CRP, WBC) are trending down, (4) no evidence of persistent biliary obstruction. Typically 3-5 days after admission. Use an 'early laparoscopic cholecystectomy' protocol

Cholecystectomy for severe pancreatitis: delay until: (1) organ failure has resolved, (2) no ongoing sepsis, (3) if necrotic collection present, it has resolved or stabilized on CT (usually 6-8 weeks). Operating in the presence of ongoing acute necrotic collection has very high morbidity and mortality. When in doubt, wait longer

ERCP technical pearls in gallstone pancreatitis: use a small sphincterotome; wire-guided cannulation reduces post-ERCP pancreatitis risk; avoid unnecessary pancreatic duct manipulation; if the stone is impacted at the ampulla, the 'needle-knife' precut sphincterotomy directly over the stone may be the safest approach; complete sphincterotomy is important for stone passage; consider pancreatic duct stent to prevent severe post-ERCP pancreatitis

Percutaneous catheter drainage for walled-off necrosis (WON): use a 12-14 Fr pigtail catheter placed via retroperitoneal approach (between left kidney and descending colon); irrigate with 50-100 mL normal saline BID; catheters may need to be upsized over time; serial CT scans to assess response; up to 40-50% of patients with infected necrosis can be managed with percutaneous drainage alone without necrosectomy

Video-assisted retroperitoneal debridement (VARD): after percutaneous drainage of WON, dilate the tract to 2-3 cm under general anesthesia; insert a laparoscope directly into the necrotic cavity; remove necrotic tissue with grasping forceps under direct vision; irrigate thoroughly; place large-bore drains (24-28 Fr) for ongoing drainage. Can be repeated if needed. Less morbidity than open necrosectomy

Open necrosectomy (when step-up approach fails or is unavailable): midline laparotomy; open the lesser sac through the gastrocolic ligament; gently bluntly dissect and remove necrotic tissue — do NOT curette or sharply dissect the pancreatic bed (risk of hemorrhage from splenic artery or SMV); fill cavity with drains placed through separate flank incisions; closed lavage with 10 L/day of dialysate; planned re-look laparotomy every 48-72h until cavity clean

Endoscopic transmural drainage of WON: EUS-guided puncture of WON bulging into the posterior gastric or duodenal wall; dilate tract with balloon (15-20 mm); place multiple double-pigtail plastic stents or a lumen-apposing metal stent (LAMS); direct endoscopic necrosectomy by passing the scope transgastrically into the cavity; repeat sessions every 3-5 days until necrosis cleared

Management of disconnected pancreatic duct syndrome: occurs when a segment of the pancreatic duct is isolated due to necrosis of the pancreatic body; leads to persistent pancreatic fluid collection (pseudocyst or WON) after resolution of acute pancreatitis; treat with endoscopic transmural drainage + long-term pancreatic duct stent (if the disconnected segment can be bridged); if endoscopic fails, distal pancreatectomy for tail disconnection

In Kenyan settings where advanced interventional options (percutaneous drainage, endoscopic drainage, VARD) are limited: (1) optimal conservative management is the foundation — IV fluids, NG decompression, enteral feeding, no prophylactic antibiotics; (2) if infected necrosis is suspected, CT-guided aspiration for culture (if CT available); (3) if surgical intervention is required (deterioration despite maximal conservative care), open necrosectomy with closed lavage and planned re-look remains the standard; (4) cholecystectomy after recovery

prognosis.mildAcutePancreatitis: 80% of all cases; mortality <1%; self-limiting with supportive care within 3-7 days; recurrence prevented by cholecystectomy (if gallstone etiology) or alcohol cessation. Full recovery without sequelae.
prognosis.moderatelySeverePancreatitis: 15% of cases; transient organ failure (<48h) or local complications; mortality 3-5%; local complications (pseudocysts, WON) resolve in 30-50% without intervention; pseudocysts >6 cm or persisting >6 weeks may require drainage
prognosis.severeNecrotizingPancreatitis: 5% of cases; persistent organ failure >48h; mortality 15-30% without infected necrosis, 30-50% with infected necrosis; requires ICU care; prolonged hospitalization (weeks to months); survivors may have exocrine insufficiency (30-40%), endocrine insufficiency/diabetes (20-40%), and long-term debility
prognosticFactors: Persistent organ failure >48h — strongest predictor of mortality (OR 10-15)
Infected necrosis — doubles mortality compared to sterile necrosis
Extent of necrosis — >50% necrosis carries mortality 30-50% vs <30% necrosis 5-10%
Age >70, comorbidities (obesity BMI >30, COPD, CKD) — increase mortality
BISAP score ≥3 at admission — predicts severe disease (mortality 5-15%)
Persistent SIRS beyond 48h — predicts organ failure in 50%
C-reactive protein >150 mg/L at 48h — sensitive for necrosis (80%)
Hemoconcentration (Hct >44% on admission that fails to decrease at 24h) — predicts necrosis
Obesity — increased risk of severe disease, organ failure, and local complications
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Late presentation (>48h from onset) is common in Kenya → higher rate of organ failure (40-50% in presented patients); mortality from severe pancreatitis is 15-25% (higher due to delayed presentation, limited ICU, and difficulty accessing ERCP/surgical expertise); gallstone etiology increasingly common; alcoholic pancreatitis still prevalent in men; hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis may be under-diagnosed; same-admission cholecystectomy for mild disease is feasible at most surgical centers

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Organ failure/necrosis -> SEVERE; mild self-limited, no organ failure -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Did the pain start suddenly in the upper abdomen?
Does the pain go through to your back?
Is the pain better when you lean forward or sit up?
Have you been vomiting?
Do you have known gallstones?
Have you had similar but milder pain before (biliary colic)?
Did the pain start after a heavy or fatty meal?
Do you drink alcohol — chang'aa, busaa, or bottled beer?
How much alcohol do you drink daily or weekly?
Have you had pancreatitis before?
Do you have a history of high triglycerides?
Are you on any medications (diuretics, steroids, immunosuppressants)?
Do you have a family history of pancreatitis?
Have you had an ERCP recently?
Have you noticed yellowing of your eyes?
Do you feel short of breath?
Are you passing urine normally?
Have you had any abdominal trauma or surgery recently?
Does the pain radiate to either shoulder (suggests diaphragmatic irritation)?
On a scale of 1-10, how severe is the pain right now?
Have you eaten a heavy or fatty meal in the last 24 hours before the pain started?
Do you have diabetes or has your blood sugar been high recently?
Have you been diagnosed with high triglycerides or high cholesterol?
Do you take any traditional herbal remedies or supplements?
Have you had a recent ERCP or endoscopic procedure?
Do you have a history of autoimmune conditions (e.g., lupus, Sjogren's)?
Have you had your gallbladder removed already?
Does anyone in your family have a history of recurrent pancreatitis?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use any tobacco products?
Is the pain constant or does it come and go in waves?
Are you passing less urine than usual?
Do you feel bloated or is your abdomen swollen?
Have you been able to pass gas or have a bowel movement?
Have you had unexplained weight loss in the past weeks?
Do you have a history of gallbladder polyps or sludge on prior scans?
Have you taken steroids or other immunosuppressant medications recently?

examination_findings.finding: Epigastric tenderness — severe on deep palpation
Guarding in the epigastrium
Absent or reduced bowel sounds — paralytic ileus
Abdominal distension from ileus or ascites
Grey-Turner sign — flank ecchymosis (retroperitoneal hemorrhage)
Cullen sign — periumbilical ecchymosis
Jaundice — if persistent CBD stone
Fever — temperature >38°C
Tachycardia >100 bpm
Hypotension <90/60
Tachypnea — respiratory rate >20
Hypoxia — SpO2 <95% on room air
Signs of dehydration — dry mucosa, reduced skin turgor
Pleural effusion — left-sided more common
Palpable epigastric mass (pseudocyst or phlegmon in late phase)
Oliguria — <0.5 mL/kg/hr urine output
Severe epigastric tenderness with guarding — Cope's sign
Abdominal rigidity — if peritonitis from ruptured pseudocyst or severe necrosis
Palpable epigastric mass — pseudocyst or phlegmon (late finding, >4 weeks)
Left pleural effusion on auscultation — dullness, decreased breath sounds
Bilateral basal crepitations — ARDS from systemic inflammation
Abdominal wall erythema or edema from retroperitoneal inflammation
Fox's sign — ecchymosis over the inguinal ligament (retroperitoneal hemorrhage inferiorly)
Positive psoas sign — pain on thigh extension (retroperitoneal irritation)
Abdominal distension measured with tape — increasing girth suggests ileus or ascites
Reduced air entry at lung bases bilaterally from splinting
Confusion or agitation — possible Wernicke encephalopathy in alcoholic pancreatitis
Signs of hypocalcemia — Chvostek sign (facial twitch), Trousseau sign (carpopedal spasm)
Necrotizing skin lesions — erythema, bullae on flanks (rare, from fat necrosis)
Subcutaneous nodules — fat necrosis from lipase release (pancreatic panniculitis)
Pitting edema of lower extremities from capillary leak and fluid overload
Hoffman sign or asterixis — if hepatic encephalopathy from co-existing alcohol liver disease
Right upper quadrant tenderness with Murphy sign — if concurrent cholecystitis or impacted stone

bedside_scores.name: Glasgow (Imrie) Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score 0-2: mild pancreatitis; 3-4: moderate; 5-8: severe pancreatitis — consider ICU

bedside_scores.name: Ranson Criteria (at 48 hours)

components.name: At admission: age, WBC, glucose, LDH, AST
At 48h: Hct drop, BUN rise, calcium, pO2, base deficit, fluid sequestration

bedside_scores.interpretation: Score 0-2: mild; 3-5: moderate; 6+: severe with high mortality

riskFactors.note: Most common cause — stones transiently obstruct ampulla
Second most common cause; chang'aa and busaa high risk
Linked to more severe disease and complications
Third most common cause; risk increases dramatically above 1000 mg/dL
Independent risk factor for acute pancreatitis
Hereditary pancreatitis (PRSS1 mutation) — very high risk

subtypes.name: Mild (Edematous/Interstitial) Gallstone Pancreatitis
subtypes.management: Conservative: NBM, aggressive IV fluids (Ringer's lactate), adequate analgesia. Oral refeeding when pain-free and tolerating fluids. MRCP if concern for persistent CBD stone. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy during same admission once pancreatitis resolves (definitive treatment to prevent recurrence).

subtypes.name: Severe (Necrotizing) Gallstone Pancreatitis
subtypes.management: ICU admission. Aggressive IV fluids (Ringer's lactate, target 5-10 mL/kg/hr). Enteral nutrition via NG tube (start within 48h). ERCP <24h if cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction. CT at 72h if no improvement. Step-up approach for infected necrosis: antibiotics → percutaneous drainage → endoscopic necrosectomy → surgical necrosectomy. Delayed cholecystectomy after recovery (6-12 weeks).

mimics.whyMimic: Acute severe epigastric pain, guarding, peritonitis
Epigastric/RUQ pain, fever, jaundice, elevated LFTs
Severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam, later peritonitis
Epigastric pain radiating to back, weight loss, jaundice possible
Right-sided abdominal pain, fever, elevated WBC
Severe epigastric/abdominal pain, back pain, hypotension

mimics.diseaseName: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) Presenting as Acute Abdomen
mimics.whyMimic: Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting in diabetic patient
Left upper quadrant/epigastric pain, fever, elevated WBC
Severe tearing pain in chest/abdomen radiating to back

laboratory.name: Serum Lipase
laboratory.indication: Gold standard — elevated >3x upper limit; stays elevated longer than amylase

laboratory.name: Serum Amylase
laboratory.indication: Elevated >3x; returns to normal faster than lipase

laboratory.name: Urea and Creatinine
laboratory.indication: BUN >20 mg/dL predicts mortality; rising Cr = renal failure

laboratory.name: Arterial Blood Gas
laboratory.indication: PaO2 <60 mmHg = respiratory failure; base deficit = severity

laboratory.name: Triglycerides
laboratory.indication: Check if no gallstones/alcohol — >1000 mg/dL = etiology

laboratory.name: Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: Differentiates infected necrosis from sterile inflammation

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Detect gallstones, CBD dilation, gallbladder pathology

imaging.name: MRCP
imaging.indication: If persistent CBD stone suspected (elevated LFTs, dilated CBD)

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast (pancreatic protocol)
imaging.indication: Assess necrosis after 72h; Balthazar scoring for severity

imaging.name: ERCP
imaging.indication: Therapeutic for CBD stones — only if cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction

management.protocolReference: IAP/APA Guidelines for the Management of Acute Pancreatitis (2023)

resuscitation: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation: Ringers Lactate (preferred) or Normal Saline
Hour 1: 20 mL/kg bolus (1-1.5 L), then 3 mL/kg/hr for 8-12 hours
Target: HR <120, MAP >65, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr, Hct <35-44%
Avoid over-resuscitation — risk of abdominal compartment syndrome
analgesia: IV Morphine 2.5-5mg PRN (titrated) or Fentanyl PCA
Avoid NSAIDs (renal risk in hypovolemic patient)
supportive: Check blood glucose 4-6 hourly — insulin sliding scale if >10 mmol/L
Check calcium and correct if low

nutrition.mild: Oral refeeding when pain-free, hunger returns, and no vomiting. Start with soft, low-fat diet. No need for routine NG/TPN.
nutrition.severe: Start enteral nutrition within 48 hours via NG or NJ tube. Polymeric formula at 20 mL/hr, advance as tolerated. Do not use TPN unless enteral route is impossible.

surgical.indication: Infected pancreatic necrosis (gas on CT, positive cultures, clinical deterioration)
surgical.step2: Percutaneous or endoscopic drainage of necrotic collections (catheter drainage)
surgical.step3: Endoscopic necrosectomy (transgastric or transduodenal) if step 2 fails
surgical.step4: Surgical necrosectomy (Video-Assisted Retroperitoneal Debridement — VARD or open) if steps 2-3 fail
surgical.timing: Delay intervention >4 weeks from onset if possible to allow walled-off necrosis formation

nonOperative: ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction: if cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy: definitive treatment for gallstone pancreatitis
Timing: during same admission for mild pancreatitis; delayed 6-12 weeks for severe necrotizing

general: Continue IV fluids guided by urine output, lactate, Hct
Enteral nutrition via NG/NJ — continue unless ileus severe
Daily CRP, WBC, calcium, glucose, creatinine
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin (once no active bleeding)
necrosectomy_specific: Monitor drain output — irrigate cavity with saline if indwelling catheters
CT if fever persists to assess for residual necrosis
Long-term: exocrine insufficiency — pancreatic enzymes; endocrine insufficiency — insulin
discharge_criteria: Plan for cholecystectomy (if mild, during same admission)
Diabetes and exocrine insufficiency management plan

pathways.criteria: Glasgow <3, no organ failure, rapidly improving, no necrosis on CT
pathways.treatment: IV fluids (Ringer's lactate), analgesia, NBM initially then oral refeeding. MRCP if LFTs persistently elevated. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy during same admission.

pathways.criteria: Glasgow ≥3, organ failure, necrosis on CT, CRP >150 at 48h
pathways.treatment: ICU. Aggressive IV fluids. Enteral nutrition within 48h. ERCP <24h if cholangitis. Step-up approach for infected necrosis. Delayed cholecystectomy at 6-12 weeks.

operative_templates.indication: Infected pancreatic necrosis — step-up approach after failed percutaneous drainage
steps: Patient supine or flank position with left side elevated 30°
Under GA, with previous drain in situ
Make 3-5 cm incision in left flank over previous drain tract
Enter retroperitoneal space using finger dissection
Insert laparoscope through the incision for visualization
Remove necrotic tissue using grasping forceps and irrigation
Gentle debridement — avoid bleeding from splenic vessels
Irrigate cavity with 3-5 L warm saline
Place large bore drains (28-32 Fr) into cavity
Post-op: cavity irrigation with saline 3-4 times daily
operative_templates.position: Supine with left side elevated or flank
operative_templates.incision: 3-5 cm left flank incision over drain tract

dischargeCriteria: Pain-free and tolerating oral diet without vomiting
Organ failure fully resolved (no respiratory, renal, or CV support)
Drains: output <20 mL/day and non-purulent (if applicable)
Blood glucose stable or diabetes management plan in place
Plan for cholecystectomy confirmed (mild pancreatitis: same admission; severe: delayed 6-12 weeks)

patient_education.explanation: Gallstone pancreatitis is inflammation of the pancreas caused by a gallstone passing through or getting stuck at the bile duct opening shared with the pancreas. This blocks the flow of pancreatic juices, causing the pancreas to digest itself. Ranges from mild (recovery in a few days) to severe (needing ICU, drains, or surgery). Removing the gallbladder prevents recurrence.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to the hospital immediately if: the pain returns or worsens, you develop fever with chills, you have difficulty breathing, you vomit persistently, you become confused or drowsy, or your abdomen becomes very swollen and tense.
patient_education.activity: Rest during recovery. After discharge, gradually increase activity over 1-2 weeks. Avoid heavy lifting for 2 weeks after ERCP or 6 weeks after surgery. If you had necrosectomy, recovery may take months. Do not drink alcohol at all during recovery and long-term.
patient_education.diet: Start with clear fluids (uji, soup, tea) then advance to soft, low-fat foods. Eat small, frequent meals. Avoid fatty, fried, and greasy foods. Avoid alcohol completely — it can trigger another attack. Good foods: porridge, rice, bread, bananas, boiled vegetables, lean chicken, fish. If you need pancreatic enzymes: take them with every meal and snack.
patient_education.medication: Take pancreatic enzyme capsules (Creon) with meals if prescribed. If diabetic: monitor blood glucose and take insulin as directed. Take pain medication as prescribed. Do not take NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) unless cleared by your doctor.
patient_education.followUp: If mild pancreatitis: schedule cholecystectomy within 2-4 weeks (during same admission). If severe: delay cholecystectomy 6-12 weeks after recovery. Repeat CT scan in 4-6 weeks if necrosis was present. Follow up with surgeon and endocrinologist if diabetes or exocrine insufficiency.

before_discharge: Plan for cholecystectomy confirmed (timing based on severity)
Pancreatic enzyme and insulin management plan if needed
Dietary advice: low-fat, small frequent meals

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic review — symptom assessment, if mild: cholecystectomy scheduling
Check blood glucose, HbA1c, nutritional status. MRCP if LFTs still elevated.
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy for mild cases. CT for severe to assess pseudocyst/necrosis resolution.
If severe: clinical review, CT to assess cyst/necrosis, delayed cholecystectomy planning
Nutritional status, exocrine/endocrine function, final follow-up

when_to_return: Vomiting or inability to keep food down
Swelling or tenderness of the abdomen worsening
